texts.text: 1. Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Separation of CO2 from natural gas and biogas is necessary to reach the required grid and fuel specifications. Several techniques based on adsorption  and  absorption,  e.g.  pressure  swing  adsorption,  water scrubbing, and amine absorption have been used for industrial removal of CO2 from various gas mixtures [1]. However, the processes are quite energy consuming and costly.

texts.text: A B S T R A C T

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In the present work, single channel tubular zeolite CHA membranes with a length of 50 cm and a membrane area of 100 cm 2 were evaluated by SEM and permeation experiments with single components and industrially relevant humid mixtures. The membranes comprised well-intergrown and smooth CHA films with a thickness of < 500 nm supported on the inside of a highly porous tube. For single component permeation, a very low SF6 permeance of 4.5 × 10 10 mol/(m 2 ‧ s ‧ Pa) was observed, which indicated nearly defect free membranes. On the contrary, the membranes displayed a very high CO2 permeance of 128 × 10 7 mol/(m 2 ‧ s ‧ Pa), which illustrated the very high permeability of the CHA pores. Finally, the membranes displayed excellent selectivity for separation of industrially relevant CO2/CH4/H2O mixtures, which was attributed to selective interaction between the CO2 molecules and the polar water molecules in the pores. The highest observed CO2/CH4 separation selectivity was as high as 198 in combination with a CO2 permeance of 14 × 10 7 mol/(m 2 ‧ s ‧ Pa) at a feed pressure of 600 kPa (including 2.2 kPa water) and 293K. The corresponding CO2 flux was 0.39 mol/(m 2 ‧ s) and the corresponding CO2/CH4  separation  factor  was  162.  The  observed  membrane  performance  was  reduced  by  concentration polarisation  due  to  the  limited  feed  flow  in  the  experimental  setup.  The  corresponding  selectivity  and  CO2 permeance corrected for concentration polarisation were as high as 236 and 16 × 10 7 mol/(m 2 ‧ s ‧ Pa), and the corrected CO2 flux was 0.44 mol/(m 2 ‧ s) and corrected separation factor was 198. An estimate showed that even at a low feed pressure of 500 kPa, it would be sufficient with as few as 64 membranes for processing of a feed of 100 Nm 3 /h raw biogas, i.e. the capacity of a typical biogas plant at a large farm, to biomethane with high purity. These results illustrated that the membranes are promising candidates for industrial separation of CO2 from e.g. natural gas and biogas.

texts.orig: Zeolite  membranes  are  ceramic  materials  with  very  well  defined pores, with potentially high thermal and chemical stability, selectivity and permeability. Chabazite (CHA) zeolite has a three-dimensional pore system with pore windows of 3.7 × 3.7 Å precisely defined by eightmembered rings  of  Si -O-  units.  The  size  of  the  pore  windows  is  inbetween  the  molecular  size  of  CO2 (3.3  Å)  and  CH4 (3.8  Å),  and consequently, this zeolite may be used to separate CO2/CH4 mixtures by molecular  sieving  [18].  In  addition,  the  zeolite  displays  selective adsorption for CO2 over CH4, which also contributes [18]. Consequently, a defect-free CHA membrane seems like an ideal candidate for industrial CO2/CH4 separation.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Membranes with tubular geometry are promising candidates for industrial applications in large scale, since compact membrane modules comprising several long membranes with large membrane area may be designed. We have developed highly permeable CHA membranes and we first reported small CHA membrane discs displaying a separation selectivity of 32 and a very high CO2 permeance of 78 × 10 7 mol/(m 2 ‧ s ‧ Pa),  much  higher  than  reported  previously,  for  the  separation  of  an equimolar  CO2/CH4  mixture  at  a  temperature  of  293  K  and  a  feed pressure  of  900  kPa  [29,30].  Then,  we  reported  10  cm  long  tubular zeolite CHA membranes with H2 and CO2 single gas permeances as high as 55 and 94 × 10 7 mol/(m 2 ‧ s ‧ Pa), respectively. Meanwhile, the SF6 single  gas  permeance  was  as  low  as  3 × 10 9 mol/(m 2 ⋅ s ‧ Pa),  which indicated that the membranes were nearly defect free. At a feed pressure of 500 kPa and a permeate pressure of 12 kPa, the observed CO2/CH4 separation  factor  was  99  with  a  CO2  flux  of  1.46  mol/(m 2 ∙ s) 1 .  The corresponding  CO2 permeance  was  60 × 10 7 mol/(m 2 ‧ s ‧ Pa)  and CO2/CH4 separation selectivity was 103 [31].

texts.orig: As shown in Table 2, researchers usually report the separation performance for dry gas mixtures, while the performance for humid gas mixtures are seldom reported. However, water vapour is usually present in industrial gas streams. Consequently, the evaluation of the separation performance for humid gas mixtures is more industrially relevant than that for dry mixtures.

However, water vapour is usually present in industrial gas streams. Consequently, the evaluation of the separation performance for humid gas mixtures is more industrially relevant than that for dry mixtures.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To the best of our knowledge, highly permeable and selective CHA membranes with an industrially relevant size and suitable for separation of industrially relevant humid gas mixtures has not been reported in the scientific literature yet. In the present work, we report the evaluation of tubular zeolite CHA membranes with a length of 50 cm and a membrane area  of  about  100  cm 2 .  The  membranes  displayed  very  high  single component  and  mixture  selectivities,  high  CO2 permeance  also  for industrially relevant humid gas mixtures and high mechanical strength. It was also shown that it would be sufficient with a low number of these large membranes for processing of raw biogas to biomethane with high purity at e.g. a large farm. Consequently, the novelty of the work rests in CHA membranes that display a combination of  the  following  highly desirable  properties;  1)  industrially  relevant  membrane  size,  2)  very high permeance, 3) very high selectivity, 4) high mechanical strength, and  finally  5)  good  performance  for  industrially  relevant  humid  gas mixtures.

texts.orig: The membranes were mounted in a stainless steel cell (see Fig. 2) for permeation experiments. Single component permeation of pure He, H2 CO2, CH4, and SF6 gas was measured at room temperature using a feed pressure of ca 110 kPa (absolute) and a permeate pressure of ca 100 kPa (absolute). The exact pressures were measured with a digital pressure gauge. The permeate flow rate was measured using a bubble flow meter.

texts.orig: 2.2. CO2/CH4 separation
texts.text: 2.2. CO2/CH4 separation

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The membranes were dried at 473K for 24 h in a flow of helium before separation experiments at 293K. A scheme of the setup used for the separation experiments is shown in Fig. 1. An equimolar CO2/CH4 mixture (purity of CO2 > 99.999% and CH4 > 99.995%) was fed to the membrane at a flow rate of 60 Ndm 3 /min (maximum in the set-up) by using a mass flow controller (Brooks instrument, SHO-RATE, USA). The separation experiments were performed at feed pressures from 300 to 1000  kPa  (absolute).  A  vacuum  pump  (Pfeiffer  Vacuum,  MVP  040) connected via a dosing valve (Pfeiffer Vacuum, EVN 116) was used to maintain a low pressure at the permeate side of the membrane. A HPLC pump (Thermo Scientific, UltiMate 3000) was used to provide water to the CO2/CH4  feed through a pipe heated to a temperature of 383K in order to vary the partial pressure of water in the feed up to 2.2 kPa (close to  saturation  at  293  K).  A  drum-type  gas  meter  (Ritter  Apparatebau GmbH) was used to measure the permeate flow and an online GC (Micro 490 GC, Agilent) was used to analyse the composition of the permeate.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Since the partial pressures in the feed varied along the membrane area, the permeance was estimated by an incremental membrane model with 10 area increments. The partial pressure at each increment was estimated from a material balance.
texts.text: Since the partial pressures in the feed varied along the membrane area, the permeance was estimated by an incremental membrane model with 10 area increments. The partial pressure at each increment was estimated from a material balance.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Concentration  polarisation  may  reduce  the  observed  membrane performance. The concentration polarisation index (CPI = nm/nb), i.e. the ratio of the mole fractions of the preferentially permeating species at the zeolite film/gas interface (nm) and in the gas bulk (nb) is a measure of the extent of concentration polarisation. In brief, the CPI was estimated by first estimating the mass transfer coefficient for the gas film above the membrane from well-known correlations for flow in pipes. In the next step, the CPI was estimated from the mass transfer coefficient, the flux and the concentrations in the gas bulk and permeate using the relation given in a previous report [30].

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3.1 . Membranes characterisation
texts.text: 3.1 . Membranes characterisation

texts.label: text
texts.orig: SEM images  recorded  near  the  two  ends  and  in  the  middle  of  a membrane are presented in Fig. 2. A continuous film composed of well inter-grown zeolite crystals with a length of < 500 nm was observed at the membrane top surface. No cracks or pinholes could be observed. A continuous film with a thickness of < 500 nm was observed at the crosssection of the membrane. The SEM images illustrate that the membrane morphology was uniform along the  entire  tube.  We  have  previously reported quite similar morphology for 10 cm long CHA tubes [30]. The images of the cross sections show that apart from some minor deposits, the support pores remained completely open.
texts.text: SEM images  recorded  near  the  two  ends  and  in  the  middle  of  a membrane are presented in Fig. 2. A continuous film composed of well inter-grown zeolite crystals with a length of < 500 nm was observed at the membrane top surface. No cracks or pinholes could be observed. A continuous film with a thickness of < 500 nm was observed at the crosssection of the membrane. The SEM images illustrate that the membrane morphology was uniform along the  entire  tube.  We  have  previously reported quite similar morphology for 10 cm long CHA tubes [30]. The images of the cross sections show that apart from some minor deposits, the support pores remained completely open.

texts.orig: Table 3 shows the single component permeance of pure He, H2, CO2, CH4, and SF6  for two membranes at 293 K measured for a feed and permeate  pressure  of  110  and  100  kPa,  respectively.  The  obtained average He, CO2 and H2 permeances were as high as 36, 128, and 94 × 10 7 mol/(m 2 ‧ s ‧ Pa), respectively. Meanwhile, the average CH4 and SF6 permeances were as low as 2.7 × 10 7 mol/(m 2 ‧ s ‧ Pa) and 4.5 × 10 10 mol/(m 2 ‧ s ‧ Pa), respectively. Consequently, the observed CO2/CH4  and H2/SF6 ideal selectivities were as high as 49 and 92 × 10 3 , respectively. The observed H2/SF6 ideal selectivity was significantly higher than that we reported for 10 cm long tubular membranes [30], and several hundred times higher than the 294 and 560 reported for CHA membranes by other groups [19,20]. The very low SF6  permeance indicated that the membranes were nearly defect free, as this relatively large molecule with a kinetic diameter of 5.1 Å [35], should only permeate through defects.

texts.orig: After the experiments with a feed of humid gas at room temperature, a dry equimolar CO2/CH4 gas mixture was fed to the membrane while maintaining the feed and permeate pressure at 1000 and 120 kPa, and after  about  5  min,  the  observed  CO2  flux  was  ca  79%  of  the  flux measured for dry gas before the experiments with humid gas and the CO2/CH4 separation factor increased slightly. Then, the membrane was flushed by a feed of 300 ml/min dry helium at room temperature and atmospheric pressure overnight. Subsequently, a dry CO2/CH4 mixture was fed to the membrane at a feed pressure of 500 kPa and permeate pressure of about 50 kPa and room temperature. The observed CO2 flux was 97% of the flux measured for dry gas before the experiments with humid gas and the CO2/CH4  separation factor increased slightly. The very quick recovery of the CO2  flux and the maintained (even slightly enhanced) separation factor indicated that water desorbed rapidly and almost completely from the membrane pores even at room temperature, which is a desirable property of the membrane. In addition, the mechanical stability of the membrane was evaluated as follows. After the initial CO2/CH4 permeation experiments, the feed was changed to a flow of pure CH4 gas at a pressure of 3.0 MPa, while the permeate pressure was maintained at 100 kPa during 20 min, i.e. the pressure difference over the membrane was 2.9 MPa. In the next step, CO2/CH4 separation experiments were repeated and identical membrane performance was observed. This indicated a high mechanical stability of the membrane.

texts.orig: Concentration polarisation may reduce the observed performance of high flux membranes as discussed in our previous work for 10 cm long CHA membrane tubes and MFI discs [30,41]. In the present work, a feed flow rate of 60 Ndm 3 /min (maximum in the setup) was used to approach industrial  conditions,  but  much  higher  flowrates  may  of  course  be applied industrially. Although the maximum flowrate in the setup was used, some concentration polarisation still occurred as indicated by a CPI < 1.0, as illustrated in Fig. 5.

texts.orig: To estimate the membrane separation performance without the effect of concentration polarisation, the observed fluxes were assumed to be directly proportional to the partial pressure at the feed side of the membrane. For instance, the observed CO2 flux was 0.91 mol/(m 2 ⋅ s) at a feed pressure of 500 kPa and a permeate pressure 48 kPa as shown in Fig. 3b. At the same conditions, the CPI was 0.76, see Fig. 5. Consequently, nm  was reduced from 0.5 to 0.38 (0.50 × 0.76) and consequently,  the  partial  pressure  of  CO2 at  the  membrane  surface  was reduced from 250 kPa to 190 kPa (500kPa × 0.38) due to the effect of concentration polarisation. Therefore, the CO2  flux corrected for concentration polarisation was 1.2 mol/(m 2 ⋅ s)  (0.91 × 250/190). Fig. 3 shows the corrected fluxes and CO2/CH4 separation factors for a feed of an  equimolar  CO2/CH4  mixture  at  a  feed  pressure  of  500  kPa  as  a function of partial pressure of water in the feed. The corrected separation  factors  are  significantly  higher,  in  the  range  18 -62%  higher, compared to the experimentally observed separation factors shown in Fig. 3. The corrected CO2  flux is 9 -31% higher and the corrected CH4 flux  is  8 -20%  lower  than  the  experimentally  observed  fluxes.  The highest corrected CO2/CH4 separation factor was as high as 194, and the corresponding corrected CO2 flux was as high as 0.34 mol/(m 2 ‧ s) when the partial pressure of water in the feed increased to 2.2 kPa. The permeances were also estimated by correcting for the effect of concentration polarisation in the incremental membrane model and the results are presented in Fig. 3. The highest corrected CO2  permeance was 74 × 10 7 mol/(m 2 ⋅ s ⋅ Pa), and the corresponding corrected CO2/CH4  separation selectivity was 103 when the partial pressure of water in the feed was zero. The corrected CO2/CH4 separation selectivity increased to as much as above 200 for partial pressures of water of > 0.5 kPa in the feed. This drastically increased selectivity may be a result of selective interaction between the CO2  molecules and the polar water molecules that adsorbs in the pores as discussed above. On the contrary, the nonpolar CH4 molecules should interact much less with the water molecules. The nearly linear decrease in CO2  permeance with increasing partial pressure of water may be an effect of reduced pore volume in the presence of water vapour. The highest corrected CO2/CH4 separation selectivity was as high as 225 at a partial pressure of water of 2.2 kPa in the feed, and the corresponding CO2 permeance was 16 × 10 7 mol/(m 2 ⋅ s ⋅ Pa). Fig. 4 also shows the corrected flux and CO2/CH4 separation factor for a feed with a partial pressure of water of 2.2 kPa as a function of feed pressure. The corrected CO2  flux and CO2/CH4  separation factor and selectivity were higher than the observed ones, for instance, the corrected CO2 flux was 0.75 mol/(m 2 ⋅ s) at a feed pressure of 1000 kPa, which was 20% higher than the observed flux. The highest corrected CO2/CH4  separation factor was 198 at a feed pressure of 600 kPa and the corresponding corrected CO2  flux was 0.44 mol/(m 2 ⋅ s). In addition, the highest corrected  CO2/CH4  separation  selectivity  was  as  high  as  236  at  a  feed pressure of 600 kPa, and the corresponding corrected CO2  permeance was 16 × 10 7 mol/(m 2 ⋅ s ⋅ Pa).

The corrected separation  factors  are  significantly  higher,  in  the  range  18 -62%  higher, compared to the experimentally observed separation factors shown in Fig. 3. The corrected CO2  flux is 9 -31% higher and the corrected CH4 flux  is  8 -20%  lower  than  the  experimentally  observed  fluxes.  The highest corrected CO2/CH4 separation factor was as high as 194, and the corresponding corrected CO2 flux was as high as 0.34 mol/(m 2 ‧ s) when the partial pressure of water in the feed increased to 2.2 kPa. The permeances were also estimated by correcting for the effect of concentration polarisation in the incremental membrane model and the results are presented in Fig. 3. The highest corrected CO2  permeance was 74 × 10 7 mol/(m 2 ⋅ s ⋅ Pa), and the corresponding corrected CO2/CH4  separation selectivity was 103 when the partial pressure of water in the feed was zero. The corrected CO2/CH4 separation selectivity increased to as much as above 200 for partial pressures of water of > 0.5 kPa in the feed. This drastically increased selectivity may be a result of selective interaction between the CO2  molecules and the polar water molecules that adsorbs in the pores as discussed above. On the contrary, the nonpolar CH4 molecules should interact much less with the water molecules. The nearly linear decrease in CO2  permeance with increasing partial pressure of water may be an effect of reduced pore volume in the presence of water vapour. The highest corrected CO2/CH4 separation selectivity was as high as 225 at a partial pressure of water of 2.2 kPa in the feed, and the corresponding CO2 permeance was 16 × 10 7 mol/(m 2 ⋅ s ⋅ Pa). Fig. 4 also shows the corrected flux and CO2/CH4 separation factor for a feed with a partial pressure of water of 2.2 kPa as a function of feed pressure. The corrected CO2  flux and CO2/CH4  separation factor and selectivity were higher than the observed ones, for instance, the corrected CO2 flux was 0.75 mol/(m 2 ⋅ s) at a feed pressure of 1000 kPa, which was 20% higher than the observed flux. The highest corrected CO2/CH4  separation factor was 198 at a feed pressure of 600 kPa and the corresponding corrected CO2  flux was 0.44 mol/(m 2 ⋅ s). In addition, the highest corrected  CO2/CH4  separation  selectivity  was  as  high  as  236  at  a  feed pressure of 600 kPa, and the corresponding corrected CO2  permeance was 16 × 10 7 mol/(m 2 ⋅ s ⋅ Pa).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The obtained CO2 permeances and CO2/CH4 separation selectivities were  higher  than  most  of  the  reported  for  CHA  membranes  in  the literature (see Table 2). The observed CO2 fluxes and CO2/CH4 separation factors in the present work were also significantly higher than those reported for polymeric membranes at comparable test conditions, where a CO2 flux of less than 0.1 mol/(m 2 ‧ s) were normally observed [5,6,42]. For instance, MTR Polaris ™ membranes displayed a maximum CO2 flux of about 0.08 mol/(m 2 ‧ s) for a feed of pure CO2 at a feed pressure of 450 kPa and atmospheric permeate pressure. Cellulose acetate membranes, which are commonly used in industry, displayed a much lower CO2 flux of about 4 × 10 3 mol/(m 2 ‧ s) and a CO2/CH4 separation selectivity of 10 -35 at a feed pressure of 350 kPa, a permeate pressure of 100 kPa and a temperature of about 298K [42].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The membranes investigated in the present work displayed higher separation factors compared to what we reported previously for 10 cm long tubular CHA membranes [30]. For dry gas, the highest observed CO2 flux was 1.46 mol/(m 2 ‧ s) in combination with a CO2/CH4  separation factor of 99 at a feed pressure of 500 kPa and a permeate pressure of 12 kPa. Higher CO2 flux in combination with a relatively high CO2/CH4 separation factor has been reported for a < 60 mm long tubular SAPO-34 membrane.  The  observed  CO2  flux  was  about  2  mol/(m 2 ‧ s)  with  a CO2/CH4 separation factor of about 60. However, the feed pressure was as high as 4.6 MPa and permeate pressure was as low as 84 kPa [23]. In addition, SAPO-34 is very sensitive to water vapour and showed poor stability in the presence of water vapour [43]. On the contrary, the much larger CHA membrane investigated in the present work performed very well even for gas saturated with water vapour, which makes it suitable for industrial applications.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4. Conclusions
texts.text: 4. Conclusions

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In the present work, tubular single-channel CHA membranes with a length of 50 cm were evaluated by SEM and permeation experiments. The  SEM  data  showed  that  the  CHA  film  was  smooth  and  wellintergrown with a thickness of less than < 500 nm. Very low SF6  permeances were observed, which indicated that the films were defect free. On the contrary, a very high single component CO2 permeance of 128 × 10 7 mol/(m 2 ‧ s ‧ Pa) was observed, which showed that the zeolite pores were  highly  permeable.  High  separation  factor  and  CO2  flux  were observed for a feed of a ternary CO2/CH4/H2O mixture. Water vapour drastically  increased  the  selectivity  and  reduced  permeability  almost linearly.  The  performance of the membrane was reduced by concentration polarisation due to the limited feed flow that could be applied in the experimental setup. Estimates showed that even better membrane performance would have been observed without the effect of concentration polarisation. The observed CO2  flux and separation factor was significantly higher than for polymeric membranes and other reported CHA membranes at comparable test conditions. This work demonstrates the  industrial  potential  of  tubular  CHA  membranes  for  CO2/CH4 separation.
